title: Same Pre-training Loss, Better Downstream: Implicit Bias Matters for Language Models

structured.summary: This paper investigates why pre-training loss alone does not fully explain downstream transfer performance in language models. It empirically and theoretically demonstrates that SGD implicitly biases models toward flatter minima, which correlates with better downstream transfer, supported by experiments on simplified datasets and a synthetic language setting with provable guarantees.

strengths.point: Novel theoretical insight that SGD implicitly biases language models toward flat minima at global optima
strengths.evidence: Theoretical analysis proving SGD noise covariance equals Hessian and SGD dynamics minimize Hessian trace on the manifold of global minimizers (Section 4).

strengths.point: Empirical demonstration that flatness correlates with downstream transfer beyond pre-training loss
strengths.evidence: Experiments show models with identical pre-training loss but different training duration, model size, or training algorithms have varying downstream accuracy correlated with Hessian trace (Section 5).

strengths.point: Use of controlled synthetic datasets with known generative models to isolate factors affecting transferability
strengths.evidence: Datasets generated from PCFG, HMM, and OPT models allow precise evaluation of transfer and flatness effects (Section 3).

strengths.point: Provable results in a synthetic Dyck language setting linking flatness to learning transferable features
strengths.evidence: Proof that among global minimizers, the flattest model learns intrinsic features and achieves better downstream performance (Section 4).

weaknesses.point: Insufficient comparison and positioning relative to prior foundational work on flatness and generalization
weaknesses.evidence: Lacks explicit discussion of seminal studies such as Keskar et al. (2017) and Hochreiter & Schmidhuber (1997) on flat minima and generalization.

weaknesses.point: Limited generalizability due to reliance on simplified synthetic datasets and small-scale models
weaknesses.evidence: Experiments do not include large-scale pretraining or realistic, diverse downstream tasks; no empirical validation that results hold for real-world LLMs (Section 3 and 5).

weaknesses.point: Narrow evaluation metrics focusing only on accuracy and Hessian trace without robustness or calibration measures
weaknesses.evidence: Downstream evaluation metrics exclude other important aspects of transfer such as robustness, calibration, or sample efficiency.

weaknesses.point: Lack of ablation on alternative optimizers and training hyperparameters
weaknesses.evidence: Only standard SGD and adversarial training compared; no exploration of Adam, RMSProp, or learning rate schedules to test generality of implicit bias claims.
Missing hyperparameter details (batch size, learning rate, epochs, model architecture) and incomplete description of Hessian trace computation.

weaknesses.point: Dense and less accessible theoretical exposition without intuitive explanations or illustrations
weaknesses.evidence: Section 4's projected gradient flow framework is mathematically involved and lacks diagrams or intuitive discussion.

weaknesses.point: Downstream tasks limited to in-distribution settings derived from synthetic data
weaknesses.evidence: No evaluation on out-of-distribution or semantically complex tasks, limiting understanding of flatness-transfer relationship in practical scenarios.

weaknesses.point: No statistical significance testing reported for empirical results
weaknesses.evidence: Correlations and accuracy improvements lack confidence intervals or hypothesis testing, weakening robustness claims.

weaknesses.point: Potential confounding between model size effects and optimization dynamics not disentangled
weaknesses.evidence: Larger models achieve flatter minima and better transfer, but causality between capacity and flatness is not clarified.
Details on construction, parameter count, and training procedure of the lookup table model are missing, limiting interpretability.

weaknesses.point: No discussion of impact of label noise or realistic data complexity on theoretical results
weaknesses.evidence: Theoretical analysis assumes noiseless synthetic data, ignoring common real-world data challenges.

questions.question: How well do the implicit bias effects toward flat minima observed on synthetic datasets generalize to large-scale pretrained language models on natural corpora?
questions.motivation: Understanding this is crucial to assess the practical relevance of the findings for real-world LLM training and transfer.

questions.question: Would alternative optimizers like Adam or learning rate schedules exhibit similar implicit bias toward flat minima and downstream transfer benefits?
questions.motivation: This matters to determine if the observed phenomena are specific to vanilla SGD or broadly applicable across common training algorithms.

questions.question: Can flatness measures beyond Hessian trace, such as scale-invariant metrics or robustness evaluations, provide a more comprehensive understanding of transferability?
questions.motivation: Exploring richer metrics may capture other aspects of model quality relevant for downstream tasks.

questions.question: How does label noise or data ambiguity affect SGD's implicit bias toward flat minima and the resulting transfer performance?
questions.motivation: Real-world data is noisy; understanding this impact is important for extending theoretical insights to practical settings.

critique_points.point_001: Insufficient comparison and positioning relative to prior foundational work on flatness and generalization. Lacks explicit discussion of seminal studies such as Keskar et al. (2017) and Hochreiter & Schmidhuber (1997) on flat minima and generalization
critique_points.point_002: Limited generalizability due to reliance on simplified synthetic datasets and small-scale models. Experiments do not include large-scale pretraining or realistic, diverse downstream tasks; no empirical validation that results hold for real-world LLMs (Section 3 and 5)
critique_points.point_003: Narrow evaluation metrics focusing only on accuracy and Hessian trace without robustness or calibration measures. Downstream evaluation metrics exclude other important aspects of transfer such as robustness, calibration, or sample efficiency
critique_points.point_004: Lack of ablation on alternative optimizers and training hyperparameters. Only standard SGD and adversarial training compared; no exploration of Adam, RMSProp, or learning rate schedules to test generality of implicit bias claims
critique_points.point_005: Insufficient methodological details hindering reproducibility. Missing hyperparameter details (batch size, learning rate, epochs, model architecture) and incomplete description of Hessian trace computation
critique_points.point_006: Dense and less accessible theoretical exposition without intuitive explanations or illustrations. Section 4's projected gradient flow framework is mathematically involved and lacks diagrams or intuitive discussion
critique_points.point_007: Downstream tasks limited to in-distribution settings derived from synthetic data. No evaluation on out-of-distribution or semantically complex tasks, limiting understanding of flatness-transfer relationship in practical scenarios
critique_points.point_008: No statistical significance testing reported for empirical results. Correlations and accuracy improvements lack confidence intervals or hypothesis testing, weakening robustness claims
critique_points.point_009: Potential confounding between model size effects and optimization dynamics not disentangled. Larger models achieve flatter minima and better transfer, but causality between capacity and flatness is not clarified
critique_points.point_010: Lookup table baseline underexplained. Details on construction, parameter count, and training procedure of the lookup table model are missing, limiting interpretability
critique_points.point_011: No discussion of impact of label noise or realistic data complexity on theoretical results. Theoretical analysis assumes noiseless synthetic data, ignoring common real-world data challenges
